narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 92% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for school grades in both Year 1 and Year 3, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 23, an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, identifying as male and Asian, and planning to attend full-time. Compared to typical students, your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles are much lower than the average around the middle of the pack, your LSAT is well below the usual 37, and your GPA trails the standard 3.2, while your income, gender, race, and full-time status align more closely with common applicants. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 school decile, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Next in importance was your LSAT score, followed by your Year 1 school decile, each contributing significantly toward the fail prediction in that order. Your middle family income and Asian racial background came after, also nudging the model toward expecting a failure, with your undergraduate GPA, full-time plans, and male gender having progressively smaller influences in the same direction. Overall, your academic performance indicators—especially those later-year grades and test score—stood out as the top concerns relative to peers who typically pass the bar. While demographics and income played roles, they were less decisive here. To improve your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim much higher than 23, as that's a key factor you can directly boost with preparation. Strengthening any upcoming academic deciles through better study habits or additional coursework could also help shift predictions. Building a stronger undergraduate record, if possible through post-bac programs, might address the GPA gap over time. Keep applying strategically, perhaps to schools with more holistic reviews, and consider bar prep resources early to build confidence. You've got potential—many overcome early hurdles with targeted effort.
